data.title: Levi's rallies on FQ1 results, but sell-side remains wary on tariff risks
data.description:
Levi Strauss & Co. (NYSE:LEVI) has sustained strong global demand growth for four consecutive quarters, while consistently expanding its customer base among the prized 18-30 demographic. With direct-to-consumer sales up by double-digits on an organic basis, the 172-year-old company delivered solid fiscal first quarter results, launching shares more than 13% higher before Tuesday’s opening bell.

The premarket gains, however, disguise Wall Street’s reluctance to remove any speed bumps to Levi’s (NYSE:LEVI) growth trajectory given the uncertain economic/tariff environment, and the company’s Southeast Asian supply chain, all of which results in “margin uncertainty that clouds the better-than-expected Q1 print,” says BofA Securities’ Christopher Nardone.

Nardone keeps his Neutral rating on Levi’s (NYSE:LEVI), as does Morgan’s Stanley’s Alex Straton as an uncertain “line of sight” keeps both on the sidelines.

“Our [full year] estimates don’t include incremental tariffs yet, but our base case assumes core margin trends slowing from here given the macro uncertainty,” says Nardone.

“We think there is even greater uncertainty at play, with the potential impacts of tariffs looming and not yet formally embedded in FY guidance,” agrees Straton.

But J.P. Morgan’s Matthew Boss sees little systemic risk to the company from tariffs given its diversified sourcing mix from 28 countries (most in Southeast Asia but with China representing ~1%), and more than half of its revenue from outside the U.S. Based on these factors, Boss estimates Levi’s (LEVI) weighted average tariff rate today at ~16% versus the 25% global brand average.

Additionally, Levi’s (LEVI) amplified pricing power today versus pre-pandemic also serves as a hedge against tariffs, leading Boss to upgrade Levi’s (LEVI) to Overweight from Neutral. CEO Michelle Gass acknowledges the company has pricing leverage given the health of the brand, but concedes that price increases will likely be “surgical.”  

Still, the volatile environment for clothing manufacturers that depend on a global supply chain still leaves Levi’s (LEVI) vulnerable to both supply shocks and changes in consumer spending trends.

“We recognize that the environment around us continues to have uncertainty, and we're focused on the things we can control,” Gass said on the company’s earnings call, pointing to Levi’s (LEVI) efforts to accelerate e-commerce sales, and expand its merchandise of dresses, outerwear, non-denim bottoms, and its Beyond Yoga category.

“Our key strategies are being brand-led, DTC-first and powering our portfolio, along with our efforts to rewire our company to be faster and more consumer-obsessed are working and are gaining momentum,” Gass added.

Still, the company’s conservative guidance seems to acknowledge potential headwinds. “While management appears to have set a low FY bar, we think sales trends likely remain challenged in aggregate, reflecting an anemic trend, and leaving the go-forward trajectory highly unclear,” said Morgan’s Straton.

Agrees BofA’s Nardone. “Given the uncertainty in today’s environment, we think it was prudent [for Levi’s] to embed an incrementally cautious outlook.”

data.title: AbbVie gets EU nod for giant cell arteritis treatment
data.description:
• AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV) said on Tuesday that the European Commission approved the marketing authorization of Rinvoq for the treatment of giant cell arteritis.
  • The approval is backed by positive data from a late stage trial where Rinvoq met the main goal of sustained remission and key secondary goals.
  • Rinvoq, the company's oral JAK inhibitor, is approved in Iceland, Liechtenstein and Norway, for the treatment of adult patients with giant cell arteritis.
  • Rinvoq also has EU marketing authorization for the treatment of radiographic axial spondylarthritis, nonradiographic axial spondylarthritis, psoriatic arthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, ulcerative colitis, Crohn's disease, as well as adults and adolescents with atopic dermatitis

data.title: Applied DNA regains compliance with Nasdaq minimum bid price rule
data.description:
• Applied DNA Sciences (NASDAQ:APDN) has regained compliance with the minimum bid price requirement set forth in Nasdaq Listing Rule 5550(a)(2).
  • As previously reported, the Co. was notified on Nov. 12, 2024, that its common stock failed to maintain a minimum bid price of $1.00 over the previous 30 consecutive business days as required by the Minimum Bid Price Requirement.
  • APDN +20% premarket to $1.56.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Marko Kolanovic: Markets are close to a ‘Trump put,’ selloff is a ‘clown show’
data.description:
The stock market is close to a “Trump put” that will push the president to change his trade policies, according to Marko Kolanovic, former J.P. Morgan chief strategist and co-head of global research.

The “Trump put,” as he said, is somewhere around 4,800 on the S&P 500 (SP500), around 260 points lower than its current trade level.

Kolanovic, who is known for his bearish convictions over the last few years, said that President Donald Trump is “starting to see that he basically lost this.”

“Trump can say he doesn’t care about markets,” he said. “But markets lead the economy.”

The president said he is firm in not planning to pause his tariff plans despite the market’s selloff, and that his goal is to fully eliminate bilateral trade deficits. U.S. stocks lost about $5T last week, after President Trump’s tariff announcements.

“It’s kind of like…a clown show and really has to be wrapped up soon,” Kolanovic said. “Because it’s not just bad for markets and the economy, it’s actually bad for Trump, too.”

He believes the markets are near the end of a “phase one” of the selloff, with the second phase happening as signs of a recession start to unwind.

“This situation, though similar in price/vol action, is fundamentally different than Covid,” he said via X.com. “It’s one-man-made. Analogy would be that in Covid we had vaccine, but didn’t want to give it out due to price negotiations. It is not as bad situation as not having the vaccine.”

data.title: Actelis Networks secures order for Orange County traffic infrastructure
data.description:
• Actelis Networks (NASDAQ:ASNS) has secured a new order intended to modernize Orange County, California's intelligent transportation system infrastructure.
  • The order, which were placed through Actelis' long-standing partner Western Systems, will provide Orange County with Actelis' advanced networking technology that enable secure, high-speed connectivity for traffic management systems across the region.
  • This deployment marks an important expansion of Actelis' footprint in California's transportation infrastructure, serving a county with over 3M residents and one of the nation's most heavily trafficked transportation networks.
  • "Our technology will help Orange County rapidly enhance its traffic management capabilities while providing the security and reliability essential for critical infrastructure operations." said Tuvia Barlev, Chairman and CEO of Actelis.
  • ASNS +12.15% premarket to $0.63.

data.title: UPS adds new ground shipping options in a move to boost freight efficiency
data.description:
United Parcel Service (NYSE:UPS) announced on Tuesday the expansion of its shipping portfolio with two new ground services. The company highlighted that the UPS Ground Saver and UPS Ground with Freight Pricing services align with the company's customer-first strategy that aims to enhance delivery options for residential and commercial shippers. 

Ground Saver is designed for less urgent packages, offering economical rates, delivery times comparable to standard UPS Ground, and features like Delivery Photo and recipient control upgrades through UPS My Choice. It targets direct-to-consumer businesses shipping lower-value merchandise while maintaining reliability.

Ground with Freight Pricing caters to shipments over 150 pounds, providing cost savings compared to less-than-truckload carriers. It eliminates additional fees for services like lift-gate delivery and offers nationwide coverage using UPS’s network. This service was said to be ideal for commercial shippers seeking reliable small package solutions at predictable rates.

UPS Chief Commercial and Strategy Officer Matt Guffey emphasized that the innovations are part of a broader plan for enhancements planned for this year, with the goal to transform the customer experience by increasing control, visibility, and choice. With the LTL market projected to reach $94.5 billion by 2025, UPS (NYSE:UPS) sees significant growth potential in offering parcel services at LTL pricing, which is seen as a unique differentiator in the industry. The company noted that the developments underscore its commitment to adapt to evolving logistics demands, while maintaining high service standards.

Shares of UPS (UPS) pushed 2.2% higher in premarket trading amid a broad market rally. The package delivery stock is still down more than 20% on a year-to-date basis. The dividend yield for new buyers is 6.85%.

data.title: Stock index futures turn green as hopes for trade negotiations rise
data.description:
Stock index futures rose on Tuesday, as tariff-driven volatility appeared to ease and hopes grew that the U.S. might enter into trade negotiations with some countries.

S&P 500 futures (SPX) +2.2%, Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) +2.1%, and Dow futures (INDU) +2.9%.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 5 basis points to 4.23%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 3 basis points to 3.80%.

U.S. stocks dropped again during Monday's trading session as ongoing tariff concerns continued to fuel uncertainty and rattle the broader market averages.

"Yet despite this morning’s recovery, markets are hardly in a good place right now, with an incredible amount of volatility still happening across different asset classes," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

U.S. President Donald Trump said Monday his administration won't pump the brakes on imposing tariffs on products coming into the U.S., but he’s open to discussing trade deals with other countries.

"Admittedly, Trump did sound open to reaching deals with other countries, saying in his message on China tariffs that 'Negotiations with other countries, which have also requested meetings, will begin taking place immediately,'" Allen added. 

On late Monday, Trump also said the European Union will have to commit to buying $350 billion of American energy to get a reprieve from the sweeping tariffs.

"Trump threatened further tax increases on U.S. consumers, with an additional 50% tax on imports from China. Rerouting supply chains becomes a viable avoidance mechanism at such levels. Japan is the first major country to negotiate with the U.S. That prioritization feels like a throwback to the trading environment of the 1980s," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

 Vietnam said it will increase its purchases of American goods, including defense and security products, as part of an effort to address its significant trade surplus with the U.S. and also requested a 45-day delay in the imposition of 46% U.S. tariffs.

data.title: HPE downgraded to Equal-weight due to pause in Juniper acquisition, tariffs: MS
data.description:
Hewlett Packard Enterprise (NYSE:HPE) was downgraded to Equal-weight from Overweight by Morgan Stanley due to its impending trial over the Juniper Networks (NYSE:JNPR) acquisition and pressure from tariffs.

"When we upgraded HPE in December, it was because we saw the close of the JNPR acquisition as imminent, which meant that there would be meaningful synergies to pass through," said Morgan Stanley analysts Meta Marshall and Mary Lenox, in a Tuesday investor note.

HPE announced its proposed $14B acquisition of Juniper in January 2024. It is expected to double HPE's networking business. The deal seemed on track to close until late January of this year, when the U.S. Department of Justice filed a complaint, labeling the deal as anticompetitive. The deal had already been approved by regulators in the U.K. and European Union. The case is slated for a bench trial on July 9.

In addition to the DOJ challenge, HPE also lowered its second quarter and full-year fiscal 2025 expectations to incorporate weaker server margins and incorporate preliminary tariffs, and the new tariffs unveiled by U.S. President Donald Trump earlier this month proved higher than expected, Morgan Stanley noted.

Morgan Stanley also lowered its price target on the stock to $14 from $24.

"The thinner margin structure of HPE, with ~30% gross margins/~11% EBITDA margins in FY26, makes tariffs tougher to absorb ... Our analysis shows that HPE has a >40% reduction in EBITA estimates when incorporating the base case tariff impact," Marshall said. "While we are estimating tariff impacts, not knowing full impacts yet, we think it will be challenging for HPE to outperform in this environment."

However, Morgan Stanley does believe the Juniper transaction will likely be approved.

"We continue to believe that the transaction getting approved is most likely, given how much bigger Cisco (CSCO) is than the combined HPE/JNPR and how the combination is far more competitive than standalone," Marshall added. "However, if DOJ ends up winning, that leaves HPE having raised debt for the transaction, but having no earnings for it."

HPE shares increased 4.2% by the close of trading on Monday. It was up another 1% during pre-market action on Tuesday.

data.title: Cass Information Systems sells telecom expense management business to Asignet
data.description:
• Cass Information Systems (NASDAQ:CASS) has entered into an asset purchase agreement to sell its Telecom Expense Management & Managed Mobility Services (TEM) business to Asignet USA.
  • The transaction is expected to close in the second quarter of 2025.
  • CASS +3.27% premarket to $41.35.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: What is stagflation?
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump's latest tariffs have led triggered a further discussion of stagflation in the economy, something that was already on the radar before "Liberation Day."

What is stagflation?

Stagflation is an economic phenomenon that is defined by periods with considerable inflation, little to no growth, and high unemployment. 

Periods of stagflation were prevalent in the 1970s and 1980s in most major economies, and the U.S. mainly battled with stagflation in the 1970s to early 1980s during the oil crisis.

The term was first coined by British politician Iain Macleod during a speech in 1965 to describe the U.K. economy.

Can Trump's tariff policy lead to stagflation?

Trump's tariff policies are expected by many to lead to elevated inflation and slow down economic growth. The policies could also lead to supply disruptions, which is one of the many causes of a stagflation.  

A supply shock would elevate prices for imported goods, which would then be passed on to businesses and consumers. Higher prices could lead to consumers tightening their purse strings and companies cutting costs -- slowing down economic growth and also potentially leading to layoffs/unemployment.
